content.text: I need to start writing documentation about how to use the new site, and separately a note on the migration from the old site to the new one. If I want to have them styled and readable in the repo but eventually suitable for both emailing and putting on the site itself, what format is best?

**MDX** is the best fit here. Since the site is Next.js (App Router), MDX files can be rendered as pages directly with no conversion step — just drop them in `src/app/` or a `content/` directory. They're readable as plain Markdown in the repo (GitHub renders them), and for email you'd run them through a Markdown-to-HTML converter (e.g. `marked` or `pandoc`) as a one-off step regardless of format.

The main tradeoff vs. plain Markdown: MDX lets you embed React components (useful if you want callouts, screenshots, or interactive bits on the site), but if you never use that, plain `.md` is simpler and has slightly wider tooling support. Given you're already on Next.js and Mantine, MDX gives you the most headroom without any cost upfront.
OK, start a migration doc in MDX
Let me check the project structure to find the right place for it.

5	const RulesPage = () => {
6	    return (
7	        <>
8	            <Flex gap="md" align="flex-start" direction="column" p="xl">
9	                <Title order={1}>Toastboy FC: Rules</Title>
10	
11	                <Text>Broadly speaking, we want a fast, competitive game with as small a
12	                    risk of injury as possible. There's no referee: we call our own
13	                    mistakes like the gentlemen we are.</Text>
14	
15	                <Text>Violent conduct is of course not allowed, and results in a
16	                    direct free kick. For safety reasons, sliding tackles and heading the
17	                    ball are also banned. Pushing and barging are not allowed: this isn't
18	                    a non-contact sport, but we do want to avoid players being bundled
19	                    into the wall/floor and breaking something.</Text>
20	
21	                <Text>The ball is not allowed over head height - infringements result
22	                    in an indirect free kick. The only exception is if the ball goes high
23	                    off the goalkeeper: if it was a deflection, we play on. If it was
24	                    deliberate, the goalie gets the ball back. Meint doesn't like this
25	                    rule, and I can see his point :-> Practicality dictates that we use
26	                    the blue line around 3 of the 4 walls in the hall as the “official”
27	                    head height, although if the ball hits the nets between the two halves
28	                    of the hall we have to call that over head height too.</Text>
29	
30	                <Text>The goalie can't deliberately leave their area (the red ‘D’),
31	                    and no other player can enter it. If a player's momentum takes them
32	                    slightly the wrong side of the line, it will be overlooked.</Text>
33	
34	                <Text>For all free kicks, defenders must be at least 2 meters away
35	                    from the ball, or as far as possible if the ball is near the goalie's
36	                    area.</Text>
37	
38	                <Text>Substitutions and goalie changes can happen at any time. By
39	                    convention, play stops whenever a player has to tie his laces or
40	                    return the ball to the clodhopping oafs next door who are always
41	                    hoofing their ball into our half. On hot days we have a quick break
42	                    half way through the game for drinks.</Text>
43	
44	                <Text>After every game, we retire to a local pub for a few
45	                    jars. Cheers!</Text>
46	            </Flex>
47	        </>
48	    );
49	};
50	
51	export default RulesPage;
52

content.text: Now create the migration doc itself. I'll put it at `src/app/footy/docs/migration/page.mdx` with a layout wrapper to keep Mantine styling:

input.content:
export const metadata = { title: 'Migrating to the New Footy Site' };

# Migrating to the New Footy Site

Welcome to the new toastboy.co.uk footy site. This document covers what has changed
from the old site and what you need to do to get started.

## Signing in

The old site used a simple email-based login. The new site uses **OAuth via Google
or Microsoft**, so you sign in with whichever of those accounts matches the email
address we already have on record for you.

If neither of those options suits you, you can request a magic-link sign-in by
email instead. Use the **Sign in** button on the home page and choose
**Continue with email**.

## Your player profile

Your stats, history, and league records have all been carried over automatically.
Your player page is at `/footy/player/<your-name>`.

If anything looks wrong — missing games, incorrect scores, or a name that is not
quite right — let Jon know and he will fix it.

## Responding to game invitations

The process is the same as before: you will receive an email before each game and
can click the link to say whether you are in or out.

On the new site you can also respond directly from your player page or from the
[next game](/footy/game) page without needing the email link.

## Ratings

You still rate other players after each game. The ratings page is linked from the
[results](/footy/results) page once the game record is published.

## What is gone

- The old `/footy/mailout` URL redirects to `/footy/players`.
- The old `/footy/nextgame` URL redirects to `/footy/game`.
- The old points/pub/stalwart/speedy table URLs all redirect to their new
  equivalents under `/footy/table/`.
- The admin pages (picker, new game, new player, responses) have moved under
  `/footy/admin/` and are only visible if you have admin access.

## Known issues

This section will be updated as issues are identified and resolved.

- None currently.

content.text:
Clean. Three files changed:

- [next.config.mjs](next.config.mjs) — wraps config with `withMDX` and adds `mdx` to `pageExtensions`
- [mdx-components.tsx](mdx-components.tsx) — maps MDX elements to Mantine components (required by App Router)
- [src/app/footy/docs/migration/page.mdx](src/app/footy/docs/migration/page.mdx) — the migration doc, accessible at `/footy/docs/migration`

The MDX file is plain Markdown so it reads well in the repo and on GitHub; Mantine styles it on the site; and for email you can run `pandoc` (or any Markdown-to-HTML tool) against it when needed. Fill in the content as you go — I've put placeholder sections for the main topics.

⨯ Error: Element type is invalid: expected a string (for built-in components) or a class/function (for composite components) but got: undefined. You likely forgot to export your component from the file it's defined in, or you might have mixed up default and named imports.

1	module.exports =
2	  (pluginOptions = {}) =>
3	  (inputConfig = {}) => {
4	    const extension = pluginOptions.extension || /\.mdx$/
5	    const userProvidedMdxOptions = pluginOptions.options
6	
7	    const mdxRsOptions = inputConfig?.experimental?.mdxRs
8	    const loader = mdxRsOptions
9	      ? {
10	          loader: require.resolve('./mdx-rs-loader'),
11	          options: {
12	            providerImportSource: 'next-mdx-import-source-file',
13	            ...userProvidedMdxOptions,
14	            // mdxRsOptions is a union of boolean and object type of MdxTransformOptions
15	            ...(mdxRsOptions === true ? {} : mdxRsOptions),
16	          },
17	        }
18	      : {
19	          loader: require.resolve('./mdx-js-loader'),
20	          options: {
21	            providerImportSource: 'next-mdx-import-source-file',
22	            ...userProvidedMdxOptions,
23	          },
24	        }
25	
26	    /**
27	     * @type {import('next').NextConfig}
28	     */
29	    const nextConfig = {
30	      ...inputConfig,
31	      webpack(config, options) {
32	        config.resolve.alias['next-mdx-import-source-file'] = [
33	          'private-next-root-dir/src/mdx-components',
34	          'private-next-root-dir/mdx-components',
35	          '@mdx-js/react',
36	          require.resolve('./mdx-components.js'),
37	        ]
38	        config.module.rules.push({
39	          test: extension,
40	          use: [options.defaultLoaders.babel, loader],
41	        })
42	
43	        if (typeof inputConfig.webpack === 'function') {
44	          return inputConfig.webpack(config, options)
45	        }
46	
47	        return config
48	      },
49	    }
50	
51	    if (process.env.TURBOPACK) {
52	      const mdxRule = {
53	        loaders: [loader],
54	        as: '*.tsx',
55	        condition: {
56	          path: extension,
57	        },
58	      }
59	
60	      // Use a unique glob string for two reasons:
61	      // - This makes it clear where the rule was defined if someone needs to debug.
62	      // - Reduces the chance of a collision (can still happen if `next-mdx` is applied twice with
63	      //   different options). Rule evaluation is ordering-sensitive, and a unique glob means we're
64	      //   less likely to break the ordering of existing rules.
65	      let wildcardGlob = '{*,next-mdx-rule}'
66	      let wildcardRule = inputConfig.turbopack?.rules?.[wildcardGlob] ?? []
67	      wildcardRule = [
68	        ...(Array.isArray(wildcardRule) ? wildcardRule : [wildcardRule]),
69	        mdxRule,
70	      ]
71	
72	      nextConfig.turbopack = {
73	        ...inputConfig?.turbopack,
74	        rules: {
75	          ...inputConfig?.turbopack?.rules,
76	          [wildcardGlob]: wildcardRule,
77	        },
78	        resolveAlias: {
79	          ...inputConfig?.turbopack?.resolveAlias,
80	          'next-mdx-import-source-file':
81	            '@vercel/turbopack-next/mdx-import-source',
82	        },
83	      }
84	    }
85	
86	    return nextConfig
87	  }
88

content.text: I'll split this into a pure-content MDX file and a `page.tsx` wrapper. Also need to check the `pageExtensions` — the standard recommendation includes `js` and `jsx`:

input.content:
# Migrating to the New Footy Site

Welcome to the new toastboy.co.uk footy site. This document covers what has changed
from the old site and what you need to do to get started.

## Signing in

The old site used a simple email-based login. The new site uses **OAuth via Google
or Microsoft**, so you sign in with whichever of those accounts matches the email
address we already have on record for you.

If neither of those options suits you, you can request a magic-link sign-in by
email instead. Use the **Sign in** button on the home page and choose
**Continue with email**.

## Your player profile

Your stats, history, and league records have all been carried over automatically.
Your player page is at `/footy/player/<your-name>`.

If anything looks wrong — missing games, incorrect scores, or a name that is not
quite right — let Jon know and he will fix it.

## Responding to game invitations

The process is the same as before: you will receive an email before each game and
can click the link to say whether you are in or out.

On the new site you can also respond directly from your player page or from the
[next game](/footy/game) page without needing the email link.

## Ratings

You still rate other players after each game. The ratings page is linked from the
[results](/footy/results) page once the game record is published.

## What is gone

- The old `/footy/mailout` URL redirects to `/footy/players`.
- The old `/footy/nextgame` URL redirects to `/footy/game`.
- The old points/pub/stalwart/speedy table URLs all redirect to their new
  equivalents under `/footy/table/`.
- The admin pages (picker, new game, new player, responses) have moved under
  `/footy/admin/` and are only visible if you have admin access.

## Known issues

This section will be updated as issues are identified and resolved.

- None currently.

import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
import type { Options } from '@mdx-js/loader'
import type { RuleSetConditionAbsolute } from 'webpack'

type WithMDX = (config: NextConfig) => NextConfig

type PluggableListName = 'remarkPlugins' | 'rehypePlugins' | 'recmaPlugins'

declare namespace nextMDX {
  interface NextMDXOptions {
    /**
     * A webpack rule test to match files to treat as MDX.
     *
     * @default /\.mdx$/
     * @example
     * // Support both .md and .mdx files.
     * /\.mdx?$/
     */
    extension?: RuleSetConditionAbsolute

    /**
     * The options to pass to MDX.
     *
     * @see https://mdxjs.com/packages/mdx/#api
     */
    options?: {
      [Key in keyof Options]: Key extends PluggableListName
        ?
            | (
                | string
                | [name: string, ...options: any[]]
                | NonNullable<Options[Key]>[number]
              )[]
            | Options[Key]
        : Options[Key]
    }
  }
}

/**
 * Use [MDX](https://github.com/mdx-js/mdx) with [Next.js](https://github.com/vercel/next.js)
 */
declare function nextMDX(options?: nextMDX.NextMDXOptions): WithMDX

export = nextMDX

content.text:
Clean. The structure is now:

- [src/app/footy/docs/migration/migration.mdx](src/app/footy/docs/migration/migration.mdx) — pure MDX content (no JS exports), readable as Markdown
- [src/app/footy/docs/migration/page.tsx](src/app/footy/docs/migration/page.tsx) — Next.js page that imports and renders it, exports `metadata`
- [mdx-components.tsx](mdx-components.tsx) — maps MDX elements to Mantine components
- [mdx.d.ts](mdx.d.ts) — TypeScript ambient declaration for `.mdx` imports
- [next.config.mjs](next.config.mjs) — wired up with `withMDX`

The page should now be live at `/footy/docs/migration`. The separation of content from routing also means when you want to email the doc, you just run `pandoc migration.mdx -o migration.html` against the MDX file directly.

## Error Type Runtime Error ## Error Message Element type is invalid: expected a string (for built-in components) or a class/function (for composite components) but got: undefined. You likely forgot to export your component from the file it's defined in, or you might have mixed up default and named imports. Check the render method of `li`. at li (mdx-components.tsx:14:31) at _createMdxContent (src/app/footy/docs/migration/migration.mdx:37:4) at MDXContent (src/app/footy/docs/migration/migration.mdx:50:3) at MigrationPage (src/app/footy/docs/migration/page.tsx:10:9) ## Code Frame 12 | ul: ({ children }) => {children}, 13 | ol: ({ children }) => {children}, > 14 | li: ({ children }) => {children}, | ^ 15 | code: ({ children }) => {children}, 16 | ...components, 17 | }; Next.js version: 16.2.9 (Turbopack)
The error pinpoints it exactly: `List.Item` is `undefined` at runtime under Turbopack's ESM handling, even though it exists in CJS. The fix is to import the sub-component directly as a named export:

input.description: Check if ListItem is a named export from @mantine/core

result.output: function

content.text: `ListItem` is a named export. Swap it in:

input.old_string: import { Anchor, Code, List, Text, Title } from '@mantine/core';
input.new_string: import { Anchor, Code, List, ListItem, Text, Title } from '@mantine/core';

content.text: Clean. Turbopack uses ESM tree-shaking, which doesn't carry static properties like `List.Item` through — the named export `ListItem` is the correct form to use here.

This implementation drops most anchor attributes (e.g., target, rel, title, className) and can unintentionally create insecure external links if target=\"_blank\" is ever used (missing rel=\"noopener noreferrer\"). Prefer forwarding props (e.g., spreading props) and, when target=\"_blank\", ensure rel is set appropriately; also avoid defaulting href to # unless that behavior is intentional.
Good catch. The fix is to spread all props and guard the `rel` when `target="_blank"`:
